title: 第 37 節
en: VI J MAMURIUS. 721 Anna ac Peranna1, Panda Cela, te Pales,' in which she is invoked together with goddesses of a similar character.

Even in the legend told of Anna, the old woman, who, when asked by Mars to procure him the love of Minerva (originally of Neria), disguised herself as a young woman, the spring, and then laughed at Mars, we can still recognise the old year and the young year, and survivals of customs once very prevalent among the Aryan nations 2. Besides this female representative of the year, Anna and Peranna, we find in ancient Pome a masculine representative also of the old year. We saw that in Germany, where the names for winter and death are masculine, the old year was often represented as an old man, not, as among the Slavonic tribes, as an old woman. The figure to be beaten or burned at the end of the year is called the Straw-man, the Death-man, or the Old Jew. In the same way the Romans celebrated their Mamuralia about the time of the vernal equinox, part of their rejoicings consisting in carrying about a man, clothed in skins, and beating him unmercifully till he was driven out of the town. This man was Mamurius, and was supposed to have made the new ancilia, when the old shields, which were believed to have originally fallen down from the sky, had been lost during the reign of Numa.

Usener identifies this festival of Mamurius with the Feriae Martis. Certain it is that the Salii, who carried the twelve shields (ancilia) in procession, 1 Peranna is the oldest authentic form instead of Perenna, and perannare is used in the sense of living one year. Perennis was originally peremnis, like sollemnis.
2 See Usener, 1. c, p. 224.
722 MAMURIUS. [chap.

invoked Mamurius Veturius at the end of their carmen saliare, according to some traditions, as a reward for the service rendered by the clever old smith Mamurius in making eleven new ancilia after the pattern of the only old ancile which remained ; but far more likely as the representative of the old or dying year. But although Mars was the god of the Salii, there seems no excuse whatever for identifying Mamurius with Mars. Marmar and Mamers can be understood as names of Mars, derived from the same root as Marut, though independent in their formation, but Mamurius was from the first meant for something not only different from Mars, but in one sense the very opposite of him, the dying year as opposed to the new year introduced by Mars. If Mamurius represented the old year, its death would take place about the same time when the new vernal god, Mars, asserted his universal sway. I venture to go even further and to suggest that the eleven ancilia which were lost and were replaced by the smith Mamurius Veturius, a kind of Ribhu, and which were carried about by the twelve ' Salii, were intended originally for the eleven months of the old year which were lost, but forged again after the pattern of the last month by the old smith or the old year. No doubt, all this was forgotten at Rome, yet some recollections seem to have survived. For what can Varro have meant when he wrote (L.L. 6, 45, p. 226), ' Itaque Salii quod can tan t Mamuri Veturi, significant veterem memoriam ' ? What can be the meaning of a passage in the Liber glossarum (cod. Vat. Palat. lat. 1773, f. 40 r), 1 Usener, 1. c, p. 226.

vi] MAMURIUS. 723 ' Ancilia, scuta anni unius ' ' ? Professor Usener, though like Corssen he identifies Mamurius 2 with Mars, is evidently of opinion that these names and the legends connected with them refer to the old and the new year. He also accepts the ancile which fell from the sky on the first of March, the natalis Martis, as the first month of the new year. If then we have recognised in Veturius Mamurius the dying year and in Mars the god of the month of March, as the beginning of the new year, we can well understand why Mars, as Prof. Roscher has so well shown, should, like Apollon, though for different reasons, have been looked upon as the god of the year, the seasons, and the months. With Apollon this was due to his solar nature, with Mars to his representing the season of the March winds (the Marutas), the return of spring, and the beginning of the new year. Hence we naturally find several points of coincidence between Apollon returning from his winter 077-0877^10, and Mars driving out the old and bringing in the new year. But the reasons are different. Mars is never supposed to have been absent among Hyperboreans, or in Aithiopia, or in hiberna Lycia ; his existence begins every year in March, and nothing is said about his whereabouts in winter. It may be admitted that Apollon and Mars had the same birthday, but their very birth had a different meaning. Apollon was born once, and the spring, though it may be called his birth, was really looked upon as his return only from a distant land. In the case of Mars, we hear of no birth in Delos or anywhere, 1 Corssen, in K. Z., ii, 55 ; Usener, 1. c, p. 213.
2 Formed like Mercurius, a purely Latin name derived from mere, in merx, mercis, but see p. 725.
724 MAMURIUS. [chap.
and it is extremely doubtful whether his so-called birthday in March was ever looked upon as his return from a far country.

There is hardly anything told of Apollon that cannot be explained by his solar character, by his opening the heavenly gates, while in the case of Mars, though there are some solar traces, they are restricted to his representing the spring and all that at Rome was connected with the spring, the festivities of the Salii, warlike expeditions and emigrations, such as the ver sacrum, &c. As the father of Komulus and the patron of the people of Rome, Mars could hardly avoid becoming a warlike deity, a Mars Gradivus rather than a Mars Silvanus, and the later identification of Mars with the Greek Ares may have helped to accentuate his warlike feature more strongly, and to give him as his companions Pallor and Pavor, like Actios and 3>o/3o5 in Greek. That the Greek Ares was ever genetically connected with Mars seems to me far more doubtful now than formerly, though Prof. Decharme has certainly brought out a number of striking coincidences between Ares and the Maruts which had escaped me when writing my Lectures on the Science of Language. Ares, however, was a Thracian rather than a Greek god, and the loss of an initial m in his name, though possible, would be very exceptional. We must leave the successful analysis of Ares to future researches. Neither his being chained during thirteen months by the Aloadai, nor his being chained by Hephaistos, allows us to see any clear physical background behind the veil of mythology. After all, the Greeks were at no time restrained from indulging in poetical fancies about their gods, vi] MAMUKIUS. 725 and a song of Demodokos, however risque, was sure to find an appreciative audience.

If Mars was originally a god of the storm, and had his name in common with the Maruts, the name of Mercurius, if like Hermes, originally a wind-god, might be a variety of Merturius, by a change analogous to that of Marcus, Marcellus, and Martellus.
There were local stories at Athens about Ares as a more peaceful deity, but these too have hitherto resisted successful interpretation.
Athene.

No goddess has caused so much controversy as Athene. She is so well known from the Homeric poems, and she has gained so many friends and admirers, that any attempt to trace her glorious features back to a more humble origin has been resisted as if it were an outrage, or even a sacrilege. But are we to make an exception in favour of the beloved daughter of Zeus, if Zeus himself and Apollon and Hermes have been made to disclose their physical antecedents ? Even if she springs full armed from the head of Zeus, is not that ' head of Zeus ' the Vedic murdha div&h, the forehead or the head of the sky, that is, the East ? The sun also is said in the Veda to have been born from the head (.sirshata/i #atam, X, 88, 16). And what goddess springs from the East, if not the Dawn, the Koryphasia of Messene, the Akria of Argos, the Agrya of the Veda, and the Capita of Rome ?
It is quite true that Homer does not relate her more miraculous birth from the head of Zeus, when laid open by the axe of Hephaistos, or of Prometheus, 726 ATHENE. [chap.

or of Hermes. But that does not prove that he or his contemporaries did not know of it, that it was in fact a post-Homeric creation. At all events he knows of no mother of Athene, and he says in II. v, 875, av yap re/ce? a(f>pova Kovprjv, 'for thou hast begotten the demented maid,' and again, II. v, 880, <xvro5 iyecvao 7rcuS' cuSt/Ao^, as if wishing to throw the whole responsibility for Athene on the shoulders of Zeus. She is called o^piiLoirdrpy), the daughter of a mighty father, and she is throughout the daughter of Zeus, just as Ushas, the Dawn, is the duhita Diva/?. If there was no other evidence, this would suffice in the eyes of any comparative mythologist to show that the prototype of Athene, however distant from the horizon of Homer, was the Dawn, born from the forehead of the sky.
Name of Athene.

But let us see once more what is the origin of her name ? It is mere accident that there should only be one passage in the Big-veda where the name of the Dawn that corresponds to Athene has been preserved. That name is Ahana, and in the only passage in which it occurs it means dawn. I have tried to show in the chapter on phonetics that the equation of Ahana = Athene is phonetically irreproachable, and I hope that I need not return again to this point. We possess fragments only of Vedic poetry as it was four thousand years ago, fragments only of Greek mythology as it was during the Homeric period. We must not demand more than we have any right to demand, and be grateful for the unexpected emergence of Ahana from the deluge that has buried so many words Vl] NAME OF ATHENE. 727 and so many thoughts of those who lived before Agamemnon and before VasisbiAa. If anybody expected to find Athene, the goddess of wisdom, the protectress of every Greek art, and of all the epya 3A0r)vaLr)<;, among the half-fledged goddesses of the Veda, he will be disappointed.

Athene having long been the protectress of Athens, naturally reflected the character of the Athenians, and not of Vedic ifoshis. She became BovAcua, presiding over the Senate, 'Ayopata, watching over the popular assemblies, 'Epyavrj and /^xa~ nns, the work-woman ; but after the victories of the Athenians, and after the erection of her statue by Phidias, seen from afar by travellers and sailors, every Athenian thought of her chiefly as the fighting-goddess, as 7to\lovxos, protectress of the town, as viK7)(f)6po<;, victorious, as Ni/o) airTepos, Victory without wings, that is, never flying away from Athens.

Whether all this could have grown out of a goddess of lightning, as Decharme seems inclined to suppose, is a difficult question. Lightning, no doubt, is a light-bringer, but it seems far too momentary a phenomenon to serve as the support of an immortal goddess. It is different with the Dawn who returns regularly every morning, who is represented not only as lighting up the whole of nature, but as waking men to a new life, as imparting to all beings the blessing of new intelligence. To be awake and to be intelligent are often expressed by the same word, and hence the rays of the morning have been compared to the bright thoughts of the mind. Why should not the same metaphor have occurred to the ancient Aryas who saw so much more in nature than -728 NAME OF ATHENE. [chap.

we are able to discover. Even the stories of Athene's relations with Helios, Hephaistos, and Herakles, conflicting as they are with her later character of the TTapdevos or the virgin, would hardly have survived if, during her purely physical stage, she had not been in close contact with these solar gods and heroes. Such incongruities are sometimes very instructive. As they run counter to the later and established character of certain gods, they may be supposed to be of an early date, and to allow us an insight into what these gods were or were considered to be before they had taken their place in the well-organised pantheon of Homer or Hesiod.
Aphrodite = Charis.

If the change of the Dawn into a goddess such as Athene requires careful proof, the metamorphosis of the same natural apparition into the goddess of beauty and love, under her numerous names, has been accepted without much demur. The old question has of course been asked, How is it possible ? How can that charming being whom the Greeks worshipped as Aphrodite have been the red blush of the morning ? And how can the same apparition be called the horse (asva or harit), the dog (sarama), the cow (go), the bird (patanga), &c. ?

All we can answer is that we have to deal with facts that may be ignored, but cannot be annihilated, and that all that the historian of the human mind can do is to accept facts and to try to understand them. When the Vedic poet says (I, 163, 2), ' Ye Vasus, you have made a horse out of the sun,' language could hardly be clearer. That the Dawn Vl] APHEODITE = CHARIS. 729 was spoken of as a cow was clearly shown before from passages of the Veda and from verses of Estonian peasants. That the red horses, the Haritas, were meant for the Dawns as companions of the sun, is no longer called in question, and that Sarama, who finds the entrance of the black stable of the night, is represented as a swift dog belonging to Indra, any one who can read the text or a translation of the hymns of the Big-veda, may see for himself. One verse will suffice to describe what the Vedic poets saw or imagined they saw every morning. 'Brihaspati drove out the cows, he split the cave by his word, he hid darkness, and made the sun to be seen.' (Rv. II, 24, 3.) Are these cows to be called Kuhfetische ? (Oldenb., Bel. des Veda, p. 207.) Or in less metaphorical language, II v. I, 157, 1 : ' Agni awoke, the sun rises from the earth, the bright, mighty Dawn opens with her splendour, the Asvins have yoked their chariot for their course, SavitW (sun) has brought forth every living thing.'

And what should we gain by calling the Dawn a Kuhfetisch ? In the Veda, the Dawn has already become a woman, a beautiful woman, arrayed in beautiful garments, dancing and displaying her charms. She appears in golden splendour, drawn by red horses, and followed by the Sun, as by a lover. I ventured many years ago to explain the name of Charis (Aphrodite) as the Greek equivalent of Harit, plur. Haritas, and I have shown again and again that phonetically no objection can be raised. But how, it has been asked again and again, could Harit, the name of the red horses of the sun, have become the name of Aphrodite ? It did not become, no Sanskrit word ever becomes a Greek 730 APHRODITE = CHARTS. [chap.
word. But both Charis and Harit were derived by the same suffix from the same root Har, to shine.

The seven horses or far-shining rays of the sun were called harit, the splendour of the dawn was likewise called harit. Splendour and brilliancy came to mean beauty and grace, y^/311?, an(^ to be bright {■yaipetv) came to mean to be glad, to rejoice. Where is there any difficulty 1 Even in the Veda the seven red horses are sometimes, when the work assigned to them is more appropriate for women, replaced by the seven sisters, ' the seven maidens, who nursed the white one, when born, the red one when growing1.' But immediately afterwards the poet continues, ' the mares came near as to a foal when born, the gods admired Agni at his birth.' Sometimes these seven sisters are called (/ami, relations, as when we read in IX, 37, 4, that Soma has brightened up the sun together with the sisters.

We must not judge Vedic poets by our own standards of taste, and we must remember that when they called the quickly-spreading splendours of the Dawn, red horses, beautiful maidens, or bright cows, these were metaphors that came natural to peasants, however strange they may sound to modern poets, and as their Dawn had not as yet assumed the sharp and settled outline of a woman like Aphrodite, they did not jar on Vedic as they would have jarred on Greek ears. Homer calls Aphrodite the Charis 2, and as such the wife of Hephaistos. This 1 See also Rv. X, 5, 5 ; 8, 3 (svasn/i arushi/t).

2 The close connection of Aphrodite and the Charites is vouchsafed by inscriptions found near the Theseion, where an altar was dedicated 'A<ppo8irr] jj-ye^oi/^ tov drjfiov koX Xiiptcnv. See Gruppe, Jahresbericht, p. 70.
zh: 六 J 瑪慕利烏斯。721 阿納及佩蘭納¹、潘達塞拉、汝帕萊斯²，在此她與同類的女神一起被祈求。

即便在安娜（Anna）的傳說中——這位老婦人曾被瑪爾斯（Mars）要求為他贏得米涅瓦（Minerva，原本是涅里亞〔Neria〕）的愛，她便偽裝成年輕女子，化身春天，隨後嘲笑瑪爾斯——我們仍然可以識認出舊年與新年，以及曾在雅利安民族中非常流行的習俗遺存2。除了這位年份的女性代表安娜與佩安娜（Peranna）外，我們在古代羅馬還發現了舊年的男性代表。我們已看到，在德國，冬季與死亡的名稱為陽性，舊年往往被表現為一位老人，而不如斯拉夫民族那般表現為老婦人。在年終被毆打或焚燒的人物被稱為稻草人、死神人（Death-man）或老猶太人。羅馬人同樣在春分時期慶祝瑪木拉利亞節（Mamuralia），其慶祝活動的一部分包括攜帶一位身著獸皮的男子遊行，並無情地毆打他，直至將他驅趕出城鎮。這位男子叫瑪穆留斯（Mamurius），據信在努瑪（Numa）統治期間，原本被認為從天上掉落下來的古老盾牌（ancilia）遺失後，他製作了新的盾牌。
烏塞納爾將馬穆里烏斯節（Mamurius）與馬爾斯節期（Feriae Martis）相等同。可以確定的是，薩利祭司（Salii）在遊行中攜帶十二面盾牌（ancilia），¹ 佩蘭納（Peranna）是比佩倫納（Perenna）更古老的真實形式，而 perannare 一詞用於「活過一年」的意義。佩倫尼斯（Perennis）原本是 peremnis，如同 sollemnis（莊嚴的）一詞。
2 見烏塞納，同上，第224頁。
722 瑪穆留斯。[章。

根據某些傳統，羅馬祭祀者在其《薩利烏斯聖歌》(carmen saliare) 的結尾處祭祀瑪穆里烏斯‧維圖里烏斯 (Mamurius Veturius)，作為對聰慧老工匠瑪穆里烏斯的酬謝，他依照現存唯一古老盾牌 (ancile) 的樣式製作了十一面新的盾牌 (ancilia)；但更有可能的是，他代表的是舊年或垂死之年。然而，儘管瑪爾斯是薩利烏斯人 (Salii) 的神祇，卻毫無正當理由將瑪穆里烏斯與瑪爾斯等同。瑪爾瑪爾 (Marmar) 和瑪梅爾斯 (Mamers) 可理解為瑪爾斯的名號，衍生自與瑪魯特 (Marut) 相同的詞根，但在其構成上各自獨立；然瑪穆里烏斯從一開始就被設定為不僅異於瑪爾斯之物，而且在某一意義上恰為其對立——垂死之年對立於由瑪爾斯引入的新年。若瑪穆里烏斯代表舊年，則其死亡應當發生在新的春季神祇瑪爾斯主張其普遍統治權的大致同一時期。我敢進一步推測，那些遺失的十一面盾牌及由工匠瑪穆里烏斯‧維圖里烏斯（一類似於里布胡 (Ribhu) 的人物）重鑄，並由十二位薩利烏斯祭祀者 (Salii) 攜帶的盾牌，本原本係為舊年的十一個月份（已遺失者）而設，但卻由老工匠或老年本身依照最後一個月份的樣式重新鍛造。毫無疑問，所有這一切在羅馬已被遺忘，然某些記憶似乎存留了下來。瓦羅 (Varro) 何以會著文寫道（《拉丁語言學》6,45，第226頁）：「因此薩利烏斯人祭祀瑪穆里烏斯‧維圖里烏斯，乃具有古老記憶之意義」(Itaque Salii quod cantant Mamuri Veturi, significant veterem memoriam)？《詞彙編纂書》(Liber glossarum)（梵蒂岡抄本Palat. lat. 1773，第40開頁）中的一段文字又作何解釋？

vi] 瑪姆瑞烏斯。723 '盾牌一年之久' ' ? 烏塞納教授雖然如科森那樣將瑪姆瑞烏斯與馬爾斯相同一，但顯然認為這些名字及與其相關的傳說指涉舊年與新年。他也接受那面從天空落下、在三月一日（馬爾斯的誕生日）的聖盾（ancile），視為新年之始的第一個月。既然我們已在韋圖里烏斯‧瑪姆瑞烏斯身上認出了垂死的舊年，在馬爾斯身上認出了三月月神（作為新年之始），我們就能很好地理解為何馬爾斯如羅舍教授所深入闡明的那樣，應該像阿波羅一樣（儘管理由各異），被視為年神、季神及月神。就阿波羅而言，這源於其太陽本質；就馬爾斯而言，則源於其代表三月風季（馬魯陀神），春天的回歸，以及新年的開始。因此我們自然而然地發現阿波羅從冬日歸來與馬爾斯驅散舊年迎來新年之間有若干巧合之點。但其理由則各不相同。馬爾斯從未被認定曾經消失於極北之地、埃塞俄比亞或冬季的呂基亞；其存在每年三月才開始，而其冬季行蹤則未有絲毫提及。可以承認阿波羅與馬爾斯有相同的誕辰，但其誕生本身有著迥異的含義。阿波羅僅誕生一次，而春天雖然可稱為其誕生，實則卻被視為其單純從遠方的歸來。在馬爾斯的情況下，我們未曾聽聞其在得洛斯或任何地方的誕生，¹ ¹ 科森著，《比較語言學雜誌》第二卷，55頁；烏塞納，同上，第213頁。
2 形成方式如同墨丘利神（Mercurius），一個純粹的拉丁名字，衍生自 mere，見於 merx、mercis，但參見第 725 頁。
724 瑪穆里烏斯。[章節
而且極其可疑的是，他所謂的三月誕辰是否曾被視為他從遙遠國度的回歸。

關於阿波羅（Apollon）幾乎沒有任何記載不能由其太陽特性來解釋，也就是他開啟天門之舉；而在戰神馬爾斯的情況下，雖然存在一些太陽痕跡，但這些痕跡僅限於他代表春季，以及在羅馬與春季相關的一切事物——薩利祭祀的慶典、戰爭遠征和移民，例如聖春（ver sacrum）等等。作為羅慕路斯的父親和羅馬人民的守護者，馬爾斯幾乎不可避免地成為一位戰神——勝利行進的戰神（Mars Gradivus）而非森林之神（Mars Silvanus），而後來馬爾斯與希臘戰神阿瑞斯的同化可能進一步強化了他的戰爭特性，並給了他蒼白女神與恐懼女神作為伴侶，就像希臘神話中的阿克提俄斯（Actios）和佛波斯（Φόβος）一樣。希臘阿瑞斯是否曾在遺傳上與馬爾斯相連，在我看來，比起從前要可疑得多了；儘管德查美教授確實揭示了許多阿瑞斯與風神馬魯特（Maruts）之間的顯著巧合，這些是我在撰寫《語言科學講座》時所遺漏的。然而，阿瑞斯與其說是希臘神，不如說是色雷斯神；且在其名字中失去起始的 m 音，雖然可能，但會是非常罕見的例外。我們必須將阿瑞斯的成功解析留待未來的研究。無論是他被阿羅亞代斯人（Aloadai）鎖鏈捆綁十三個月，或是被火神赫淮斯托斯鎖鏈束縛，都不能讓我們看到神話面紗背後有任何明確的物理背景。畢竟，希臘人在任何時代都未曾受到約束，不能對其諸神沉溺於詩意的幻想，而狄摩多科斯（Demodokos）的歌曲，儘管大膽風趣，也必然會獲得欣賞的聽眾。
倘若馬爾斯（Mars）原本是風暴之神，其名與馬魯特神（Maruts）相通，則墨丘利（Mercurius）之名，若如赫爾墨斯（Hermes）一樣源自風神，便可能是梅爾圖里烏斯（Merturius）的一個變體，其變化類同於馬庫斯（Marcus）、馬塞盧斯（Marcellus）及馬爾泰盧斯（Martellus）之變化。
雅典曾有關於戰神阿瑞斯（Ares）作為較和平之提婆的地方性故事，但這些故事迄今為止仍未得到成功的詮釋。
雅典娜。

沒有任何女神像雅典娜（Athene）一樣引起如此多的爭議。她在荷馬詩篇中名聲遠揚，並且贏得了眾多摯友與仰慕者，以至於任何將她光彩奪目的特徵溯源於更卑微的起源的嘗試，都會遭到抗拒，彷彿那是種冒犯，甚至是褻瀆。但如果宙斯本身、阿波羅與赫爾墨斯都已被迫披露了他們的物質來源，我們豈可為所鍾愛的宙斯之女破例呢？縱然她全副武裝地從宙斯之首誕生，那「宙斯之首」難道不就是吠陀中的 murdha div́h（天空之額或天空之頭），也就是東方嗎？在吠陀中，太陽據說也是從頭部（śīrṣatáh śatám, X, 88, 16）誕生的。那麼，若非曙光——墨西涅的 Koryphasia、阿爾戈斯的 Akria、吠陀的 Agrya，以及羅馬的 Capita——還有什麼女神會從東方誕生呢？
確實如此，荷馬並未敘述她更為奇蹟般的誕生——即從宙斯的頭顱中誕生，而宙斯的頭顱是由赫菲斯托斯或普羅米修斯的斧頭劈開的，726 雅典娜。[chap.

或赫密斯的故事。但這並不能證明荷馬本人或他的同時代人並不知曉此說，亦不能證明它事實上並非荷馬後期的創作。無論如何，他對雅典娜的母親一無所知，他在《伊利亞特》第二卷第875行說：「蓋汝曾生育癲狂之少女」（av yap re/ce? a(f>pova Kovprjv），又在第880行說：「他本人生出此無瑕疵之子女」（avTo5 iyecvao 7rcuS' cuSt/Ao^），彷彿欲將雅典娜之全部責任推諸宙斯肩上。她被稱為「強大之父之女」（o^piiLoirdrpy)），她始終是宙斯之女，正如曙光女神烏莎（Ushas）乃是天空之女（duhita Diva/?）。倘若別無其他證據，這一事實在任何比較神話學家眼中已足以證明：雅典娜的原型，無論距離荷馬的視野多麼遙遠，乃是從天空之額頭誕生的曙光。

In the mythology of the Greeks, Athene is represented as the goddess of wisdom, and as such we find her name explained in various ways by the ancient commentators. Some derive it from *athesis*, signifying "without mother" (*a* and *thesis*, mother); others from the same root as *theos*, "god"; others, again, from *aithein*, "to burn"; others from *aithra*, "ether"; and others from *ate*, "mischief" or "infatuation." It would be difficult to decide between these etymologies, and it is far more likely that the name is of pre-Aryan origin, and that the etymologies attempted by the ancient Greeks are merely guesses. 在希臘神話中，雅典娜被描繪為智慧女神，因此我們發現古代評論家以各種方式解釋她的名字。有些人從 *athesis*（意為「無母」，*a* 和 *thesis* 意為母親）推導而來；有些人則從與 *theos*「神」相同的詞根推導；又有些人從 *aithein*「燃燒」推導；有些人從 *aithra*「乙太」推導；還有些人從 *ate*「惡行」或「迷癡」推導。在這些詞源學解釋中難以做出抉擇，而且遠為可能的是，這個名字源於前雅利安起源，古代希臘人所嘗試的詞源學解釋不過是推測而已。

但讓我們再看一次她的名字起源是什麼呢？梨俱吠陀中只有一個段落保存了與雅典娜相應的黎明女神的名字，這純屬巧合。那個名字是阿哈那（Ahana），在它出現的唯一段落中意為黎明。我在音韻學一章中試圖說明阿哈那=雅典娜（Ahana = Athene）的語音對應在語音學上是無懈可擊的，我希望不必再次回到這一點。我們只擁有四千年前吠陀詩歌的片段，只擁有荷馬時期希臘神話的片段。我們不應要求超過我們有權要求的範圍，而應為阿哈那從淹沒了許多詞語和許多阿伽門農與婆私吒之前那些人的思想的洪流中意外浮現而感恩。如果有人期望在吠陀的半成熟女神中找到智慧女神、保護者雅典娜，以及保護所有希臘事業（ἔργα Ἀθηναῖα）的女神，他將大失所望。

雅典娜長期以來一直是雅典的保護女神，自然而然地反映了雅典人的性格，而非吠陀時代的仙人（ṛṣis）的性格。她成為了 Boulaia（議會的守護者），'Αγορaia（監護人民集會），'Εργανη 及 Pallas（手工藝的女工）；但在雅典人取得勝利之後，以及在菲迪亞斯為她樹立的雕像落成之後——這尊雕像遠遠可見，為旅人和水手所瞻仰——每位雅典人主要將她視為戰神（Πολιουχος），城鎮的保護者，為 Nikephoros（勝利者），為 Nike Apteros（無翼勝利），也就是永遠不會離開雅典的勝利。

無論這一切是否可能源於一位閃電女神，如德夏爾（Decharme）似乎傾向於假設的那樣，這是一個困難的問題。閃電無疑是帶來光明者，但它似乎是一種過於短暫的現象，無法成為一位不朽女神的支撐。晨曦則不同，她每天早晨都有規律地歸來，不僅被描繪為照亮整個自然界，而且被描繪為喚醒人類迎接新的生命，為所有生靈賦予新的理性祝福。清醒與聰慧往往用同一個詞來表達，因此晨曦的光芒被比作心靈的光亮思想。為什麼同樣的隱喻不可能出現在看到自然界如此之多的古代雅利安人身上，他們——

我們能夠發現。即使是雅典娜與赫利俄斯、赫淮斯托斯和赫拉克勒斯的故事，雖然與她後來的貞女（TTapdevos）特性相互矛盾，但倘若在她純粹物質性的階段中，她未曾與這些太陽神祇和英雄有過密切接觸，這些故事也不太可能得以流傳至今。這類矛盾之處有時是極具教益的。由於它們與某些神祇後來既定的特性相悖，我們可以推測它們來自於早期，使我們得以窺見這些神祇在進入荷馬或赫西俄德精心組織的萬神殿之前，本為何物或被認為為何物。
阿佛洛狄忒（Aphrodite）= 卡里斯（Charis）。
如果把黎明變化為雅典娜（Athene）這樣的女神需要謹慎的證明，那麼同一自然現象變化為美與愛之女神的過程，儘管她有眾多名稱，卻已被接受而幾無異議。當然，古老的疑問隨之而起：這如何可能？希臘人崇拜為阿佛洛狄忒（Aphrodite）的那個迷人的存在，怎麼可能是晨曦的紅潤面容？同一自然現象又怎麼能被稱為馬（asva 或 harit）、狗（sarama）、牛（go）、鳥（patanga）等？

我們所能回答的只是：我們必須面對的是一些事實，這些事實可以被忽視，卻不能被消滅；而人類心靈的歷史學家所能做的，就是接納這些事實，並試圖理解它們。當吠陀詩人說（I, 163, 2）：「汝等提婆，汝等自日輪造出一馬」時，語言再也不能更清楚不過了。曙光被稱為一頭母牛，這一點早已從吠陀的篇章及愛沙尼亞農民的詩句中清楚地顯示出來。那紅色的馬匹，哈里塔斯（Haritas），是為曙光們所備，作為太陽的伴侶，這一點已不再有人質疑；而薩拉瑪（Sarama）尋得黑夜的黑色馬廄的入口，被表現為屬於因陀羅（Indra）的迅捷獵犬，任何能夠讀懂原文或梨俱吠陀讚歌譯文的人，都能親眼看到。一句詩文足以說明吠陀詩人每個清晨所看到或想像他們看到的東西：「婆哈斯帕蒂（Brihaspati）驅趕出母牛們，他以其言語裂開山洞，他隱沒黑暗，使太陽得以被看見」（Rv. II, 24, 3）。這些母牛是否應稱為牛的崇拜（Kuhfetische）？（奧登堡，《吠陀宗教》，207頁。）或用較少比喻的語言，梨俱吠陀第I卷，157，1：「阿格尼（Agni）甦醒了，太陽自大地升起，光明而強大的曙光以其光輝開展，雙馬童（Asvins）為其行程套上了戰車，薩維特爾（Savitri，太陽）生成了萬物眾生。」

我們若將曙光稱為牛魅（Kuhfetisch），又能獲得什麼呢？在吠陀中，曙光已經化身為女性，一位美麗的女性，身著華美的衣裳，翩翩起舞，展現其魅力。她以金色的光彩顯現，由紅馬駕駛，被太陽追隨，如同被情人追隨。多年前，我曾冒險將希臘女神卡里斯（Charis，即阿佛洛狄忒）的名字解釋為梵文 Harit 及其複數形式 Haritas 的等價物，並一再證明在語音學上不能提出任何異議。但人們一再提問，Harit 這個太陽之紅馬的名字，怎樣會成為阿佛洛狄忒的名字呢？它並未成為，沒有任何梵文詞彙曾經成為希臘文詞彙。
字詞。但是 Charis 和 Harit 都是由同一個字根 Har（閃耀）加上相同的字尾所衍生的。

太陽的七匹馬或遠閃的光芒被稱為 harit，黎明的光輝同樣被稱為 harit。光輝和光彩逐漸演變為美麗和優雅，而變得光亮（yaipetv）遂演變為感到歡喜、感到高興。這裡有什麼困難嗎？即使在吠陀中，七匹紅馬有時候，當賦予它們的工作更適合女性時，也會被七姐妹所取代——「七位少女，當白者（太陽）誕生時給予撫養，當紅者（太陽）成長時照顧它」。但詩人隨即繼續說道：「母馬們靠近了如同新生的駒馬，眾神在阿格尼誕生時對他表示崇敬。」有時這七位姐妹被稱為 (ami——親戚，如同我們在《梨俱吠陀》第九卷第三十七讚歌第四節所讀到的，索摩與眾姐妹一起使太陽光輝燦爛。

我們不應該以自己的品味標準來評斷吠陀詩人，必須記住，當他們把黎明快速展開的光輝稱為紅馬、美麗的少女或光亮的牛隻時，這些對於農民來說是自然而然的隱喻，儘管對現代詩人耳中聽來奇異無比。由於他們的黎明還未曾如同阿芙蘿迪蒂（Aphrodite）那樣承擔起尖銳而確定的女性輪廓，這些隱喻並不會如同在希臘人耳中那樣顯得刺耳，而在吠陀人耳中則不然。荷馬稱阿芙蘿迪蒂為卡里斯（Charis），並以此作為赫菲斯托斯（Hephaistos）的妻子。¹ --- ¹ 參見《梨俱吠陀》X, 5, 5；8, 3（svasn/i arushi/t）。
阿佛洛狄忒與美惠三女神（The Charites）之間的密切關聯，由提塞翁附近發現的銘文所證實，該處有一座祭壇獻給「Ἀφροδίτη μεγάλη τοῦ δήμου καὶ Χάριτες」。參見格魯佩著《年度報告》第70頁。
